hook: You already know how to find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix, and you've seen that multiplying by a matrix can stretch, rotate, or reflect vectors. But what if you could find a special coordinate system where a matrix just scales each axis independently — turning a complicated matrix into a simple diagonal one? The question is: when is that possible, and how do you actually find it?
concept: Diagonalization is the process of writing a square matrix $A$ as a product $A = PDP^{-1}$, where $D$ is a diagonal matrix and $P$ is invertible. In plain English, you change coordinates so the transformation becomes pure scaling. Formally, an $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is diagonalizable if and only if it has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors; those eigenvectors form the columns of $P$, and the corresponding eigenvalues fill the diagonal of $D$.
anatomy: The diagonalization $A = PDP^{-1}$ has three distinct components. The matrix $P$ is called the modal matrix or eigenvector matrix — its columns are $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors of $A$, ordered consistently with $D$. The matrix $D$ is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues, where the entry $D_{ii} = \lambda_i$ corresponds exactly to the eigenvector in column $i$ of $P$; the ordering of eigenvectors and eigenvalues must match. The matrix $P^{-1}$ is simply the inverse of $P$, which exists precisely because the eigenvectors are linearly independent, guaranteeing $P$ is invertible.

worked_example.description_latex: We start with the matrix $A$ that we want to diagonalize. Our plan is to find its eigenvalues, then its eigenvectors, then assemble $P$ and $D$ — so every later step builds directly on this matrix.

We find the eigenvalues by solving the characteristic equation $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$. Expanding the $2 \times 2$ determinant and factoring gives us two distinct eigenvalues, $\lambda_1 = 5$ and $\lambda_2 = 2$, which tells us right away that $A$ is diagonalizable because two distinct eigenvalues for a $2 \times 2$ matrix guarantee two linearly independent eigenvectors.

For each eigenvalue we solve $(A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ to find a corresponding eigenvector. Notice that both systems reduce to a single equation with a free variable, giving us one eigenvector per eigenvalue; we pick simple integer representatives to keep the arithmetic clean.

We assemble $P$ by placing $\mathbf{v}_1$ in column 1 and $\mathbf{v}_2$ in column 2, and $D$ by placing the matching eigenvalues along the diagonal in the same order. The column-eigenvalue pairing is critical: if you swap a column of $P$ without swapping the corresponding entry of $D$, the factorization $A = PDP^{-1}$ will be wrong.

Finally, we compute $P^{-1}$ using the $2 \times 2$ inverse formula $\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix}d&-b\\-c&a\end{pmatrix}$, where $\det(P) = -2-1=-3$. Multiplying $PDP^{-1}$ and recovering $A$ confirms the diagonalization is correct — always worth a quick check.

partial_example.description_latex: We want to diagonalize $B$. Start by finding the eigenvalues: compute $\det(B - \lambda I) = (3-\lambda)(-1-\lambda) - 0 = 0$. Because the matrix is lower-triangular, the eigenvalues are simply the diagonal entries, $\lambda_1 = 3$ and $\lambda_2 = -1$.

Now find an eigenvector for each eigenvalue by solving $(B - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$. Set up and row-reduce both systems, then write down $\mathbf{v}_1$ (for $\lambda_1 = 3$) and $\mathbf{v}_2$ (for $\lambda_2 = -1$).

Using the eigenvectors just found, we assemble $P$ (columns = eigenvectors) and $D$ (diagonal = matching eigenvalues). The eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 3$ is $\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\end{pmatrix}$ and for $\lambda_2 = -1$ is $\begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix}$.
Compute $P^{-1}$ and then verify the diagonalization by multiplying out $PDP^{-1}$ and checking that you recover $B$. Write down $P^{-1}$ and the product $PDP^{-1}$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Is } A = \begin{pmatrix}2 & 0\\0 & 5\end{pmatrix} \text{ diagonalizable? If so, state } P \text{ and } D.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Yes. } P = I = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix},\ D = \begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&5\end{pmatrix} \text{ (already diagonal; any ordering of eigenvectors works)}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Use diagonalization to compute } A^{10} \text{ where } A = \begin{pmatrix}4&1\\2&3\end{pmatrix}. \text{ (Use the result } A=PDP^{-1} \text{ from the worked example.)}
\text{Explain why } A = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 1\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix} \text{ is NOT diagonalizable over } \mathbb{R}.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{The only eigenvalue is } \lambda=1 \text{ (repeated). Solving }(A-I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}\text{ gives eigenspace } \text{span}\left\{\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}\right\}\text{, which is 1-dimensional. Since we cannot find 2 linearly independent eigenvectors, }A\text{ is not diagonalizable.}

common_mistakes: Students mix up the ordering of columns in $P$ and entries in $D$: if $\mathbf{v}_1$ is placed in column 2 of $P$, then $\lambda_1$ must also appear in position $(2,2)$ of $D$ — mismatching them breaks the identity $A = PDP^{-1}$ entirely. Always double-check that column $i$ of $P$ pairs with diagonal entry $i$ of $D$.

Students forget to check whether the matrix is actually diagonalizable before proceeding: when an eigenvalue is repeated, its geometric multiplicity (dimension of the eigenspace) must equal its algebraic multiplicity, or no diagonal form exists. Assuming diagonalizability without verifying this leads to a system with no solution when you try to form $P$.

Students compute $P^{-1}AP$ instead of $PDP^{-1}$ when verifying, confusing which side $P^{-1}$ belongs on. Remember that $A = PDP^{-1}$ means $P^{-1}AP = D$, so both expressions are related but serve different purposes — the former recovers $A$ from $D$, while the latter recovers $D$ from $A$.
untested_variants: Diagonalizing a $3 \times 3$ matrix with three distinct eigenvalues, which requires row-reducing three separate $(A - \lambda_i I)$ systems.

Determining diagonalizability when an eigenvalue has algebraic multiplicity 2 but geometric multiplicity 2 (so the matrix IS diagonalizable despite a repeated eigenvalue), as opposed to the defective case in practice problem 5.
Using the diagonalization $A = PDP^{-1}$ to compute $e^A$ (the matrix exponential) by the formula $e^A = Pe^DP^{-1}$, where $e^D$ has $e^{\lambda_i}$ on the diagonal.
Diagonalizing a symmetric matrix using an orthogonal matrix $P$ (i.e., orthogonal diagonalization via the Spectral Theorem), where $P^{-1} = P^T$.
